mappings.rationaleAI: Mandela as the co-founder of the ANC Youth League, leader of armed resistance, 27-year prisoner, and first Black president of South Africa (1994) following the negotiated end of apartheid directly addresses the Republic of South Africa's antiapartheid movements, legalization of the ANC, and establishment of black majority rule with the ANC's victory in 1994 all-race elections (E.8.b.i).

Egypt's coverage of British occupation, post-1952 republican governance, conflicts with Israel (1948-1973), and the Camp David Accords addresses Egypt from 1922 to 1945 including Wafd-led opposition to British presence (B.2) and radicalization of Egyptian politics, the 1952 revolution, Nasser, the Suez Crisis, Six-Day War, and Sadat's presidency (B.3.e).

Nigeria's coverage of British colonization, unification of protectorates in 1914, independence in 1960, the Biafra civil war, military dictatorships, and democratic governance from 1999 addresses West Africa since c. 1920 (E.4), decolonization and independence including formation of African independence movements and post-independence political instability (E.4.b).

Iran's coverage from the Pahlavi monarchy through the 1979 Islamic Revolution, the Iran-Iraq War, and authoritarian governance directly addresses Iran since 1925 (C.1), the regime of Reza Shah including reforms and WWII Allied invasion (C.1.a), Mohammad Reza Pahlavi's regime including the Mosaddeq premiership and oil nationalization (C.1.b), and the 1978-79 revolution establishing the Islamic Republic under Khomeini and the Iran-Iraq War (C.1.c).

Israel's establishment in 1948, contested jurisdiction over the West Bank and Gaza, and the ongoing Arab-Israeli conflict directly address the partition of Palestine and establishment of Israel (B.3.c) and the establishment of Israel and resultant conflicts with the Arabs including immigration, hostilities, the Suez War, Six-Day War, and peace processes (B.3.c.i).

The Ottoman Empire's dissolution into the Turkish Republic and its role in shaping 20th-century Middle Eastern state formation provides essential background context for Turkey since 1919 (A), as the war for independence and Ataturk's reforms emerged directly from the Ottoman collapse.

Algeria's French colonial annexation (1830-1962) and the bloody independence struggle address French colonial policies and the Algerian War of Independence (D.1.a). Post-independence governance including the civil war (1991-2002) addresses the emergence of independent Algeria under Ben Bella and subsequent regimes (D.2.d).

Ethiopia's coverage of the Kingdom of Aksum, Haile Selassie's imperial rule, Italian occupation (1936-1941), the Soviet-backed Derg junta (1974-1991), and Eritrean independence directly addresses Ethiopia and Eritrea since 1917 including Italian conquest, federation with Eritrea, the military government abolishing the monarchy, and Eritrean independence (E.5.a).

Tanzania's formation in 1964 from Tanganyika and Zanzibar, CCM one-party governance, and colonial history through German East Africa and British trusteeship directly addresses Tanzania (formerly Tanganyika and Zanzibar) including the Zanzibar revolt, Nyerere and ujamaa socialism, and economic reforms (E.6.b.iii).

Saudi Arabia's unification under the Al Saud dynasty in 1932, hydrocarbon-dependent economy, and Wahhabi governance directly address the emergence of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia under Ibn Saud, oil discoveries and exploitation, and increasing control of oil resources (B.4.a).

Turkey's coverage of Ataturk's secular republic from 1923, the Ottoman imperial legacy, tension between secularism and Islamic political revival, and NATO membership directly addresses Turkey since 1919 (A), the war for independence and Ataturk's leadership (A.1), Ataturk's one-party government and reforms (A.2), and Turkey since 1938 including postwar developments and political changes (A.3).

South Africa's coverage of Dutch and British colonization, the National Party's apartheid regime (1948-1994), the ANC-led liberation struggle, and 1994 universal elections directly addresses Southern Africa since c. 1920 (E.8), political developments in white-settler-controlled Union of South Africa (E.8.a), and the Republic of South Africa including apartheid, antiapartheid movements, and establishment of black majority rule (E.8.b.i).

Kenya's coverage of the British East Africa Protectorate, the Mau Mau uprising, independence in 1963, and successive Kenyatta presidencies directly addresses Kenya including independence, presidency of Kenyatta, one-party state under Moi, and election of Kibaki (E.6.b.v).

The DRC's coverage of Belgian colonialism, independence in 1960, Mobutu's Zairian dictatorship, and the Congo Wars directly addresses the Democratic Republic of the Congo including the Congolese nationalist movement, independence, secession of Katanga, UN intervention, and the regime of Mobutu (E.7.a.i).

WWII's relevance to Turkish wartime neutrality until alignment with the Allies (A.3.a), Egypt's participation in World War II (B.2), and postwar changes in colonial policies leading to the decline of the colonial system and rise of African nationalist parties (E.3) is directly addressed.

The British Empire's Scramble for Africa, World Wars, and decolonization address the completion of effective occupation by European powers and post-WWI division of German colonies (E.1), administrative policies and attitudes of each colonial power (E.2), and postwar changes in colonial policies and the decline of the colonial system (E.3).

France's colonial empire and its decolonization connect to French colonial policies, the French protectorate in Morocco, administration of Algeria, and the Algerian War of Independence (D.1.a), as well as Lebanon and Syria under the French mandate (B.1.a).

Italy's Fascist period connects to Libya under the Fascist Italian government from 1922 to 1942 (D.1.b). Italy's conquest and occupation of Ethiopia (1936-1941) connects to Ethiopia and Eritrea since 1917 including Italian conquest (E.5.a).

The Cold War's proxy wars and ideological competition connect to postwar changes in colonial policies and the decline of the colonial system amid superpower rivalry (E.3). The US-sponsored military coup ousting Mosaddeq in Iran (1953) exemplifies Cold War intervention (C.1.b).

WWI's dissolution of the Ottoman Empire directly led to Turkey's war for independence (A.1). The post-war mandate system in Arab lands of Southwest Asia (B.1) and the post-WWI division of German colonies among colonial powers (E.1) are direct consequences of WWI.

Spain's territories in North Africa (Ceuta, Melilla) and colonial possessions connect to the Spanish Zone in Morocco (D.1.a) and Morocco's dispute including the Spanish Zone and its reduction to Ceuta and Melilla (D.2.c).
NATO's establishment in 1949 and Turkey's membership connect to Turkey since 1938 including postwar alignment, relations with East and West, and Turkey's role as a NATO member (A.3).

The Middle East as a geopolitical region encompassing the Arabian Peninsula, Egypt, Iran, Iraq, the Levant, and Turkey, with coverage of the Arab-Israeli conflict, petroleum economies, and colonial legacy, broadly addresses the development of the Arab states and Israel (B) and the Arab states, Egypt, and Israel since 1945 (B.3).

Decolonisation as the process of colonial territories achieving independence from European powers directly addresses postwar changes in colonial policies and the establishment of independent African countries (E.3), decolonization and independence in West Africa (E.4.b), and the establishment of independent states in the Maghrib (D.2).

Colonialism as the practice of extending political, social, and economic domination over territory directly addresses the completion of effective occupation by European powers and the mandate system (E.1), and administrative policies and attitudes of each colonial power and effects on African societies (E.2).

The Soviet Union's dissolution into fifteen independent states connects to the Central Asian republics under Soviet rule from 1917 and independence after Soviet collapse in 1991 (C.3). Soviet Cold War rivalry and its intersection with Turkey's postwar problems with the Soviet Union connects to Turkey's wartime neutrality and postwar alignment (A.3.a).
